One is from an allied nation, and one is a US medic. The US medic is unconscious and not breathing. A buddy performed CPR before moving him. The allied solider is conscious, with large open wounds on both legs. He has a tourniquet on his right leg above the knee. The injuries on his left leg are noticeably bleeding but not spurting.

decision_question: Who do you examine and assess first?

base.scenario: They are at an accident collection point at the site of a building collapse after an earthquake. The protocol for this multi-agency/multi-organization response contains clear indications that no volunteer who is not reviewed by any of the organizations is allowed to provide medical treatment on site. Although you are associated with one of the organizations, some papers confusion causes your recent certification not to show in the system so that you have been released to monitor the accident collection site, but have not been released to actually medical treatment. The time for medical transport to a hospital is uncertain due to road and infrastructure disturbances, but the current estimate is that no transport will arrive for at least an hour. One of the victims had a severe blood loss that was checked during the first triage. They have now displayed symptoms of shock and fluid recovery. The two medical providers on site have not been able to get a IV successfully started, and none of them has used an intra-osseous access method. They are not comfortable to do it for the first time in the field. They have used this method to get off for 20 years.

items.situation_text: Let them step in to do the IO access now.
Send them to the command site to get vetted.
Step in and perform the IO access yourself.
Verbally coach one of the on-site medical providers on how to do the IO access.

base.scenario: They monitor two victims in a temporary accident collection point, waiting for medical evacuation. One victim has intense pain due to extensive burns and the other has intense pain from a crushing injury to her foot. They report similar levels of pain and you would like to treat them both with an oral painkiller, but you only have an OTFC lozenge available. There is a policy that you should never share doses of an oral medication. You know this is because some medications have a time-release coating that is deactivated by cleavage, but you are sure that OTFC lozenges do not have such a coating.

items.situation_text: Ignore the policy. Split the dosage, to treat both patients.
Obey the policy. Do not split the dosage. Treat the patient with burns.
Obey the policy. Do not split the dosage. Treat the patient with a crush injury.

base.scenario: You have a patient in the medical tent who has already received IV antibiotics for a persistent skin infection (cellulitis). They have been monitored here for a week and are eager to get back to work. Mail from overseas has been disturbed, and today is the first time care packages have arrived in weeks. Your patient, buddy brought them only one package from home, full of their favorite junk food. There is a policy that patients who stay in the medical tent are not allowed to eat any food except for the meals provided. You know this is because some conditions may be exacerbated by certain foods, but you are sure that cellulitis would not be influenced by snacks.

items.situation_text: Ignore the policy. Let them eat the junk food.
Obey the policy. Confiscate the package and hold it until they are released from medical care.
Obey the policy. Ask their friend to take the package back to their tent.

decision_question: Do you confiscate the package and prevent the patient from eating outside food while they’re under treatment in the medic tent?

base.scenario: During a patrol mission at night, your unit comes under fire, and a soldier is severely injured. You are not able to go, so must be carried in a litter. You rate it as immediately, need medical evaluation quickly. Your commander asks you what medical evacuation option would you recommend.

items.situation_text: The pre-designated helicopter landing zone, through a forest. This location is 2 miles away and the helicopter would be fastest. You have no direct way to contact the helicopter, so you would pass the evacuation request through your base as an intermediary. You are not familiar with routes through the forest. If the evacuation request is not received, or if you get lost in the forest, the patient will likely die. But this is the fastest option if things go according to plan.

Go 4 miles across some fields to meet up with a paved road. This is farther away. Once there, vehicle transportation would be slower than the helicopter. You could directly contact your base to dispatch the vehicle and coordinate the meet-up, so you are also certain that transport will be available. This option is slower but more reliable.

base.scenario: They are a medical device used in a submarine. They are called to respond to a chemical explosion in the area where equipment maintenance needs are stored. When you arrive there, an emergency door is closed, sealing the compartment where the explosion occurred. A maintenance specialist tells you that he came out of the compartment outside the door automatically closed to get help for his colleague who was seriously burned to face and neck. You know that there is a risk of toxic vapours from whatever chemical explodes, or from damage to nearby containers. The maintenance specialist tells you that nothing else has been damaged or leaked, and that the solvent that explodes is not toxic.

items.situation_text: Get a gas mask, close the door leading into this area, and then open the emergency door and enter the compartment. This will allow you to treat the injured submariner sooner, but risks at least a local release of toxic fumes.

Wait for the chemical/biological/radiation response team to come and install a mobile airlock entrance before entering the compartment with a gas mask. This will delay treatment for the injured submariner but reduce the risk of toxic fume release.

base.scenario: They treat victims at a forward medical support station as part of a continued operation against an insurgent group nearby. base.scenario: You are triaging patients after a shooting at a university. You see one unconscious patient with severe hemorrhage, and recognize that he is the student responsible for the attack at the university. You see a second patient, a victim of the attack, with multiple gunshot wounds that may not be survivable. You do not have time to treat both the shooter and the victim, and both are likely to die without treatment. The shooter’s injuries are definitely treatable, but the victim may die even if treated, so there is a higher likelihood that you can save the shooter’s life than the victim’s.

base.scenario: They work in an emergency room in a small town with limited resources. They have a room available when 2 patients arrive. Both were in a busy parking lot when one of them tore a handbag from an elderly woman. The second ran after the thief to try to recover the bag, and as they fought they fell into the way of an incoming car. The thief took more of the impact and has a collapsed lung and possible internal bleeding. The person who tried to recover the handbag has a broken collarbone and broken wrist, as well as signs of concussion.

The second ran after the thief to try to recover the purse, and as they struggled, they fell into the path of an oncoming car. The thief took more of the impact and has a collapsed lung and potential internal bleeding. The person who tried to recover the purse has a broken collarbone and fractured wrist, as well as signs of a concussion.

base.scenario: They are the medical provider for an outdoor adventure tour group during a wilderness trip. Two participants get into an argument who lost a piece of equipment and one suddenly starts throwing blows, on the edge of a cliff. Another participant sees the danger and steps in to try to calm things down. During training, both the attacker and the group member who falls across the edge intervened. A rescue helicopter can take one person out, but the other must be transported by other members of the group via stretcher, which will take much longer. The person who started the fight has a composite break with bleeding that will be difficult to control during a stretcher evacuation, and can die before reaching a point where an ambulance could hit the group. The person who tried to land the case on the person who started the fight and is less severely injured, but can suffer internal injuries that are not obvious.

You start judging a person lying on the sidewalk outside, and find that they need to be transported to the hospital in the nearest city. As you load them into the ambulance, your partner tells you that there is another patient in the store who is in the same serious condition. Your partner also says that the police told them that the patient you loaded is an armed robber who shot the employee in the store.

You have a long-term patient you have been treating for two months as they were severely burned while destroying evidence that they had stolen money from work. The next step in the treatment is a transplant with artificial skin, and this lengthy procedure is planned for today. While you are preparing to go to the treatment room with this patient, your reception stops you to ask if you can take an urgent case out of the emergency room. A teacher was cut to the face while a child was prevented from an accident, and although the wound was connected, they need seams to prevent scarring.

base.scenario: Because of staffing shortages, your hospital can conduct only one surgery at a time, even though you have several ORs available. A patient with a chronic, potentially fatal condition is scheduled for a complicated surgery today that should take about 3 hours. The surgery has been delayed many times in the past because of staff shortages. Although the patient is stable today, they are showing early signs and symptoms of liver failure, and they will not survive without the surgery for much longer. The patient has been taken to the OR and anesthetized when ambulances arrive with 3 patients, all of whom have life threatening injuries that need surgery to survive. You believe that all 3 patients could be operated on in the next 90 minutes and would survive; however, they would likely die if they have to wait more than 2 hours for surgery.

base.scenario: You work in a rural clinic and pharmacy. You have a patient you have treated for a life-threatening infection. Your infection was completely resistant to antibiotics of the first line, and responded only slightly to the second line antibiotics. They planned to start treatment with a reserve antibiotic today. Due to a widespread lack of this reserve antibiotic, your clinic has only one dose left. You will receive a call from a rural clinic an hour away. You have a patient with a life-threatening infection that has not responded at all to antibiotics of the first or second line, and will probably die without treatment with a reserve antibiotic. Due to the lack, they have no doses of reserve antibiotic, and they have already called any place within 200 miles.

items.situation_text: Give the other clinic the dose
Use the dose to treat your patient
